book_claims.claim: Green beans are part of the traditional Italian diet used as an example for the ancestral-diet approach to autoimmune disease prevention
book_claims.mechanism: Component of a vegetable-rich traditional diet associated with low rates of autoimmune and chronic disease

book_claims.claim: Beans are a vegetable staple used in multiple Longevity Diet dishes including the Molochio recipe pasta e vaianeia and Ligurian minestrone, served at ~150 g per serving
book_claims.mechanism: High fiber and plant protein content; low glycemic index; supports gut microbiome diversity and satiety

supplementary_research.finding: PMID 31728499: Higher legume intake is associated with reduced all-cause mortality and cardiovascular mortality across multiple cohort studies; beans are among the most studied longevity-associated foods
PMID 26853923: Legume consumption is associated with lower fasting blood glucose and improved insulin sensitivity; supports metabolic health as a longevity pathway
Green beans provide fiber, vitamin C, vitamin K, and folate; fermentable fiber feeds beneficial gut bacteria, supporting the gut-longevity axis

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber; supports gut microbiome and bile acid recycling
Water-soluble; brief cooking preserves more than prolonged boiling
Fat-soluble; absorption enhanced by co-ingestion with olive oil
Essential for DNA methylation; bioavailability ~85% from cooked vegetables
Cofactor for SOD2 antioxidant enzyme; plant sources have moderate bioavailability

synergies.description: Olive oil enhances absorption of fat-soluble vitamin K in beans and contributes anti-inflammatory polyphenols
Classic longevity combination in pasta e vaianeia; legume fiber lowers the glycemic response of pasta
Garlic's organosulfur compounds and beans' fiber act synergistically on gut microbiome diversity and immune modulation
Traditional pairing in Calabrian cooking; vitamin C in tomatoes enhances iron absorption from beans
